{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:C tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Prefectural Cultural Properties in Okayama]] [[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Okayama]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[sn:umano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[en:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)|12=draft}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:C tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Gōsha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Prefectural Cultural Properties in Okayama]] [[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Okayama]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[sn:umano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[en:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)|12=draft}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:C tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Prefectural Cultural Properties in Okayama]] [[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Okayama]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[sn:umano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[en:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:C tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Prefectural Cultural Properties in Okayama]] [[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Okayama]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[sn:umano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[en:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)}}

revision.comment: 47 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林) up to rev 102231695
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=熊野神社 |所在地=岡山県倉敷市林684 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}} |祭神=伊邪那美神、伊邪奈岐神、家都御子神、速玉之男神 |創建=大宝元年（701年） |社格=郷社 |例祭= |本殿=入母屋造（第三殿）、春日造（第一殿・第二殿）、流造（第四殿・第五殿・第六殿） |別名=日本第一熊野十二社権現宮 |画像=[[ファイル:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
社殿 }} [[ファイル:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|手前から第三殿（入母屋造）、第一殿（春日造）、第二殿（春日造）、第四・五・六殿（流造）]] [[ファイル:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|第二殿（国の重要文化財）]] '''熊野神社'''（くまのじんじゃ）は、[[岡山県]][[倉敷市]][[郷内地区]]にある[[熊野神社]]。「日本第一熊野十二社権現宮」と称する孝謙天皇が天平神護元年（765年）の紀州行幸の際「日本第一大霊験所」との称号を下したのは本家である紀州の[[熊野三山]]のひとつである[[熊野速玉大社]]であり、岡山県の熊野神社に対してではない。。祭神は[[イザナミ|伊邪那美神]]、[[イザナギ|伊邪奈岐神]]、[[熊野権現|家都御子神]]、速玉之男神。社殿は左から第三殿・第一殿・第二殿・第四殿・第五殿・第六殿と並ぶ[[熊野本宮大社]]と似た形式を採っている。 == 概要 == [[修験道]]の祖と言われる[[役小角]]は[[文武天皇]]3年（[[699年]]）、朝廷より訴追を受け、熊野本宮に隠れていたが[[伊豆大島]]に配流された（[[続日本紀]]）。社伝によれば、この際、義学・義玄・義真・寿玄・芳玄ら5人の弟子達を中心に熊野本宮大社の御神体を捧持したとされる。彼らは3年にわたり各地を放浪し、役小角が赦免となった[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]元年（[[701年]]）3月、神託を得て現在の地に[[紀伊国|紀州]]熊野本宮を遷座したとされる。[[天平]]12年（[[740年]]）[[聖武天皇]]が[[児島]]一円を熊野神社の社領として寄進した。[[天平宝字]]5年（[[761年]]）には紀州熊野と同様の社殿（十二社権現宮）を整え、付近の木見に新宮を、山村に那智宮（現・[[由加神社本宮]]、[[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]]）を建て新熊野三山とした。 熊野神社と修験道の寺院が一体となった[[神仏習合]]の形態を取る宗教施設として栄えたが、[[平安時代]]中期以降は衰微した。 [[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）[[承久の乱]]が勃発し、敗れて[[隠岐国|隠岐]]に遠島となった[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]に連座して、上皇の第4皇子[[頼仁親王]]が児島に配流となった。頼仁親王は衰退していた熊野神社と寺院を再興し、以後、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝]]の頃まで繁栄した。 [[室町時代]]になり[[応仁の乱]]が勃発すると、この地も戦乱に巻き込まれ[[応仁]]3年（[[1469年]]）には[[細川勝元]]方に加担した覚王院円海を中心とした兵により新熊野は焼き討ちにあい、ほぼ全焼した。[[明応]]元年（[[1492年]]）より焼き払われた建造物の再建が開始された。現在、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている第二殿はこの時造営されたものである。現在見られる第二殿を除く社殿は[[正保]]4年（[[1647年]]）に[[池田光政]]によって造営された。 [[明治時代]]になると[[神仏分離|神仏分離令]]により、十二社権現は熊野神社となり他の寺院（[[五流尊瀧院]]）は[[天台宗]]寺院となった。 [[平成]]15年（[[2003年]]）9月、[[明和]]5年（[[1768年]]）建造の拝殿（修験道では「長床」と呼ぶ）を失火により全焼[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html 岡山の風・郷土史（熊野神社の年表）]。平成19年（[[2007年]]）10月に再建した。 == 文化財 == ===重要文化財=== *第二殿：明応元年（1492年）建造。春日造。[[大正]]10年（[[1921年]]）4月30日指定。 ===岡山県指定重要文化財=== *第一殿・第三殿・第四殿・第五殿・第六殿：正保4年（1647年）建造。[[昭和]]43年（[[1968年]]）4月19日指定。 ===岡山県指定記念物（史跡）=== *新熊野山：昭和48年（[[1973年]]）5月15日指定。 == アクセス == {{Commons|Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} *[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[瀬戸大橋線]] [[植松駅]]より南西へ徒歩20分、[[木見駅]]より北へ徒歩25分 *[[下津井電鉄|下電バス]] 熊野神社入口（林）バス停より南東へ徒歩7分 *[[瀬戸中央自動車道]] [[水島インターチェンジ|水島IC]]下車 東へ約5分 == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991年 102-103ページ *現地説明板 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 外部リンク == *[http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ 日本第一熊野神社] {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:くまのしんしや くらしきしはやし}} [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:倉敷市の神社]] [[Category:岡山県の旧郷社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:倉敷市の重要文化財]] [[Category:岡山県指定重要文化財]] [[Category:岡山県指定史跡]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:江戸時代の建築]]

(倉敷市)|蓮台寺]]）を建て新熊野三山とした。 熊野神社と修験道の寺院が一体となった[[神仏習合]]の形態を取る宗教施設として栄えたが、[[平安時代]]中期以降は衰微した。 [[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）[[承久の乱]]が勃発し、敗れて[[隠岐国|隠岐]]に遠島となった[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽上皇]]に連座して、上皇の第4皇子[[頼仁親王]]が児島に配流となった。頼仁親王は衰退していた熊野神社と寺院を再興し、以後、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝]]の頃まで繁栄した。 [[室町時代]]になり[[応仁の乱]]が勃発すると、この地も戦乱に巻き込まれ[[応仁]]3年（[[1469年]]）には[[細川勝元]]方に加担した覚王院円海を中心とした兵により新熊野は焼き討ちにあい、ほぼ全焼した。[[明応]]元年（[[1492年]]）より焼き払われた建造物の再建が開始された。現在、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている第二殿はこの時造営されたものである。現在見られる第二殿を除く社殿は[[正保]]4年（[[1647年]]）に[[池田光政]]によって造営された。 [[明治時代]]になると[[神仏分離|神仏分離令]]により、十二社権現は熊野神社となり他の寺院（[[五流尊瀧院]]）は[[天台宗]]寺院となった。 [[平成]]15年（[[2003年]]）9月、[[明和]]5年（[[1768年]]）建造の拝殿（修験道では「長床」と呼ぶ）を失火により全焼[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html 岡山の風・郷土史（熊野神社の年表）]。平成19年（[[2007年]]）10月に再建した。 == 文化財 == ===重要文化財=== *第二殿：明応元年（1492年）建造。春日造。[[大正]]10年（[[1921年]]）4月30日指定。 ===岡山県指定重要文化財=== *第一殿・第三殿・第四殿・第五殿・第六殿：正保4年（1647年）建造。[[昭和]]43年（[[1968年]]）4月19日指定。 ===岡山県指定記念物（史跡）=== *新熊野山：昭和48年（[[1973年]]）5月15日指定。 == アクセス == {{Commons|Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} *[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[瀬戸大橋線]] [[植松駅]]より南西へ徒歩20分、[[木見駅]]より北へ徒歩25分 *[[下津井電鉄|下電バス]] 熊野神社入口（林）バス停より南東へ徒歩7分 *[[瀬戸中央自動車道]] [[水島インターチェンジ|水島IC]]下車 東へ約5分 == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991年 102-103ページ *現地説明板 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 外部リンク == *[http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ 日本第一熊野神社] {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:くまのしんしや くらしきしはやし}} [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:倉敷市の神社]] [[Category:岡山県の旧郷社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:倉敷市の重要文化財]] [[Category:岡山県指定重要文化財]] [[Category:岡山県指定史跡]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:江戸時代の建築]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:C tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Prefectural Cultural Properties in Okayama]] [[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Okayama]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[sn:umano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[en:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|32|25.0|N|133|49|11.3|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki City Hayashi)|qq=draft}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]

Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:C tier 1]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Prefectural Cultural Properties in Okayama]] [[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Okayama]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] }} [[sn:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[sn:umano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[en:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]]

It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the [[Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Kumano Shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Go-sha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Prefectural Cultural Properties in Okayama]] [[Category:Prefectural Historic Sites in Okayama]] [[Category:Muromachi period architecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] }} [[sn:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[sn:umano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[en:Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)]] [[Category:倉敷市の神社]] [[Category:倉敷市の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama]]
