The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[:en:Ikeda Mitsumasa|Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[:en:Uematsu Station|Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[:en:Kimi Station|Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. [[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568940]] Wikidata name: Kumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = [[:en:34|34]] | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[:en:Oki Province|Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[:en:Ikeda Mitsumasa|Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[:en:Uematsu Station|Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[:en:Kimi Station|Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[:en:Oki Province|Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[:en:Ikeda Mitsumasa|Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[:en:Uematsu Station|Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[:en:Kimi Station|Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] }} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568940]] Wikidata name: Kumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = [[:en:34|34]] | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[:en:Oki Province|Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[:en:Ikeda Mitsumasa|Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[:en:Uematsu Station|Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[:en:Kimi Station|Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] }} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[:en:Oki Province|Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[:en:Ikeda Mitsumasa|Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[:en:Uematsu Station|Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[:en:Kimi Station|Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[:en:Uematsu Station|Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[:en:Kimi Station|Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568940]] Wikidata name: Kumano-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = [[:en:34|34]] | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama]] city of [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]] in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == [[:en:En no Gyōja|En no Gyōja]], considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to [[:en:Izu Ōshima|Izu Ōshima]] (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to [[:en:Oki Province|Oki Province]], his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under [[:en:Ikeda Mitsumasa|Ikeda Mitsumasa]]. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from [[:en:Uematsu Station|Uematsu Station]] or 25-minute walk north from [[:en:Kimi Station|Kimi Station]] on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[:en:Seto-Chūō Expressway|Seto-Chūō Expressway]] == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama}} city of {{ill|Kurashiki|en|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487|lt=Kurashiki}} in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}}, [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}}, considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to {{ill|Izu Ōshima|en|Izu Ōshima|WD=Q1070580|lt=Izu Ōshima}} (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|WD=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū War}} broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to {{ill|Oki Province|en|Oki Province|WD=Q1063829|lt=Oki Province}}, his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under {{ill|Ikeda Mitsumasa|en|Ikeda Mitsumasa|WD=Q3148401|lt=Ikeda Mitsumasa}}. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from {{ill|Uematsu Station|en|Uematsu Station|WD=Q7877451|lt=Uematsu Station}} or 25-minute walk north from {{ill|Kimi Station|en|Kimi Station|WD=Q6410088|lt=Kimi Station}} on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the {{ill|Seto-Chūō Expressway|en|Seto-Chūō Expressway|WD=Q11566530|lt=Seto-Chūō Expressway}} == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from {{ill|Uematsu Station|en|Uematsu Station|WD=Q7877451|lt=Uematsu Station}} or 25-minute walk north from {{ill|Kimi Station|en|Kimi Station|WD=Q6410088|lt=Kimi Station}} on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the {{ill|Seto-Chūō Expressway|en|Seto-Chūō Expressway|WD=Q11566530|lt=Seto-Chūō Expressway}} == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|en|34|WD=Q3905910|lt=34}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama}} city of {{ill|Kurashiki|en|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487|lt=Kurashiki}} in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}}, [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja}}, considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to {{ill|Izu Ōshima|en|Izu Ōshima|WD=Q1070580|lt=Izu Ōshima}} (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|WD=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū War}} broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to {{ill|Oki Province|en|Oki Province|WD=Q1063829|lt=Oki Province}}, his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under {{ill|Ikeda Mitsumasa|en|Ikeda Mitsumasa|WD=Q3148401|lt=Ikeda Mitsumasa}}. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from {{ill|Uematsu Station|en|Uematsu Station|WD=Q7877451|lt=Uematsu Station}} or 25-minute walk north from {{ill|Kimi Station|en|Kimi Station|WD=Q6410088|lt=Kimi Station}} on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the {{ill|Seto-Chūō Expressway|en|Seto-Chūō Expressway|WD=Q11566530|lt=Seto-Chūō Expressway}} == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:qq]]

[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] [[Category:sn_crud commons:Category:Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)]][[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kumano Shrine | location = 684 Hayashi, Kurashiki City, Okayama Prefecture | coordinates = {{ill|34|en|34|WD=Q3905910|lt=34|1=34}} | deity = Izanami-no-Mikoto, Izanagi-no-Mikoto, Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami, Hayatama-no-O-Kami | founded = First year of Taihō (701 CE) | type = Gōsha (village shrine) | festival = | Main Hall = Irimoya-zukuri (Third Hall), Kasuga-zukuri (First and Second Halls), Nagare-zukuri (Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls) | alternative names = Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kumano Jinja 09.jpg|250px]]
Shrine buildings }} [[File:Kumano Jinja 14.jpg|220px|thumb|From the front: Third Hall (Irimoya-zukuri), First Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Second Hall (Kasuga-zukuri), Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls (Nagare-zukuri)]] [[File:Kumano Jinja 10.jpg|200px|thumb|Second Hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] '''Kumano Shrine''' (熊野神社) is located in the {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama Prefecture}} city of {{ill|Kurashiki|en|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487|lt=Kurashiki|1=Kurashiki}} in the [[Gōnai district]]. It is referred to as the "Nihon Daiichi Kumano Jūnisha Gongen-no-Miya" (Japan's Number One Kumano Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace).The title "Japan's Number One Great Spiritual Sanctuary" was bestowed by Emperor Kōken in the first year of Tenpyō-Jingo (765 CE) during her pilgrimage to Kumano Nachi Taisha in Kii Province, not the Kumano Shrine in Okayama Prefecture. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}}, [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Ketotsu-Miko-no-Kami]], and Hayatama-no-O-Kami. The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}}, considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to {{ill|Izu Ōshima|en|Izu Ōshima|WD=Q1070580|lt=Izu Ōshima|1=Izu Ōshima}} (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|WD=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū War|1=Jōkyū War}} broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to {{ill|Oki Province|en|Oki Province|WD=Q1063829|lt=Oki Province|1=Oki Province}}, his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under {{ill|Ikeda Mitsumasa|en|Ikeda Mitsumasa|WD=Q3148401|lt=Ikeda Mitsumasa|1=Ikeda Mitsumasa}}. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from {{ill|Uematsu Station|en|Uematsu Station|WD=Q7877451|lt=Uematsu Station|1=Uematsu Station}} or 25-minute walk north from {{ill|Kimi Station|en|Kimi Station|WD=Q6410088|lt=Kimi Station|1=Kimi Station}} on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the {{ill|Seto-Chūō Expressway|en|Seto-Chūō Expressway|WD=Q11566530|lt=Seto-Chūō Expressway|1=Seto-Chūō Expressway}} == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

The arrangement of the shrine halls, from the Third Hall on the left to the Sixth Hall on the right, mirrors the layout of [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]]. == Overview == {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|WD=Q198946|lt=En no Gyōja|1=En no Gyōja}}, considered the founder of [[Shugendō]], faced charges by the imperial court in the third year of Emperor Monmu’s reign (699 CE) and took refuge at Kumano Hongū before being exiled to {{ill|Izu Ōshima|en|Izu Ōshima|WD=Q1070580|lt=Izu Ōshima|1=Izu Ōshima}} (as recorded in the [[Shoku Nihongi]]). According to the shrine's legend, during his exile, five disciples (Gigaku, Gigen, Gishin, Jushin, and Hōgen) carried the sacred objects of Kumano Hongū Taisha. After wandering for three years, they received a divine revelation and enshrined the objects at the present location of Kumano Shrine in the first year of Taihō (701 CE). In the 12th year of Tenpyō (740 CE), Emperor Shōmu donated the Kojima area as the shrine’s estate. In the fifth year of Tenpyō Hōji (761 CE), similar shrine buildings (Twelve Shrines Gongen Palace) were constructed. Two additional shrines, Shingū in Kimii and Nachi-no-Miya in Yamamura, were built, forming the "New Kumano Three Shrines." The site flourished as a combined [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretic) religious institution but declined after the mid-Heian period. In the third year of Jōkyū (1221 CE), the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|WD=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū War|1=Jōkyū War}} broke out. Following Emperor Gotoba's defeat and exile to {{ill|Oki Province|en|Oki Province|WD=Q1063829|lt=Oki Province|1=Oki Province}}, his fourth son, Prince Yorihito, was also exiled to Kojima. Prince Yorihito restored the declining Kumano Shrine and its associated temples, leading to its prosperity until the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under {{ill|Ikeda Mitsumasa|en|Ikeda Mitsumasa|WD=Q3148401|lt=Ikeda Mitsumasa|1=Ikeda Mitsumasa}}. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from {{ill|Uematsu Station|en|Uematsu Station|WD=Q7877451|lt=Uematsu Station|1=Uematsu Station}} or 25-minute walk north from {{ill|Kimi Station|en|Kimi Station|WD=Q6410088|lt=Kimi Station|1=Kimi Station}} on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the {{ill|Seto-Chūō Expressway|en|Seto-Chūō Expressway|WD=Q11566530|lt=Seto-Chūō Expressway|1=Seto-Chūō Expressway}} == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed. "New Edition: Okayama Prefecture History Walk" Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 102–103. * On-site informational plaque. == Notes == {{Footnotes help}} == External Links == * [http://www.kumano-jinjya.com/ Official site of Kumano Shrine] {{Shinto Yokai}} {{translated page|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|version=102231695|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] {{Commons category|Kumano-jinja (Kurashiki)}} {{wikidata link|Q11568940}} [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kurashiki]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} reached this region. In the third year of Ōnin (1469 CE), New Kumano was burned down by forces led by Enkai of Kakōin Temple, allied with Hosokawa Katsumoto. Reconstruction began in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). The Second Hall, designated a [[National Important Cultural Property]], was constructed during this period. The remaining halls, except the Second Hall, were rebuilt in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE) under {{ill|Ikeda Mitsumasa|en|Ikeda Mitsumasa|qid=Q3148401|lt=Ikeda Mitsumasa|1=Ikeda Mitsumasa}}. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the [[Shinbutsu-bunri|separation of Buddhism and Shinto]] transformed the Twelve Shrines Gongen into Kumano Shrine, while the remaining temples (such as Gorūsontaki-in) became [[:en:Tendai Buddhism|Tendai Buddhist]] temples. In September of the 15th year of Heisei (2003 CE), a fire destroyed the worship hall, known as the "Nagataka" in Shugendō practices.[http://www.hosocom.jp/rekisi/kumano/goryu-nenpu.html Historical timeline of Kumano Shrine (Okayama no Kaze)] It was rebuilt in October of the 19th year of Heisei (2007 CE). == Cultural Properties == === National Important Cultural Property === * Second Hall: Built in the first year of Meiō (1492 CE). Kasuga-zukuri style. Designated on April 30, 1921. === Okayama Prefectural Important Cultural Property === * First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Sixth Halls: Built in the fourth year of Shōhō (1647 CE). Designated on April 19, 1968. === Okayama Prefectural Historic Site === * New Kumano Mountain: Designated on May 15, 1973. == Access == * 20-minute walk southwest from {{ill|Uematsu Station|en|Uematsu Station|qid=Q7877451|lt=Uematsu Station|1=Uematsu Station}} or 25-minute walk north from {{ill|Kimi Station|en|Kimi Station|qid=Q6410088|lt=Kimi Station|1=Kimi Station}} on the [[JR Seto-Ōhashi Line]] * 7-minute walk southeast from Kumano Shrine Entrance (Hayashi) bus stop on [[Shimo-Den Bus]] * Approximately 5 minutes east from the [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the {{ill|Seto-Chūō Expressway|en|Seto-Chūō Expressway|qid=Q11566530|lt=Seto-Chūō Expressway|1=Seto-Chūō Expressway}} == References == * Okayama Prefectural High School History Society, History Division / Ed.
